name: Shenandoah — Load Reference Barriers
summary: Red Hat / OpenJDK's Shenandoah is a concurrent compacting collector that performs object relocation in parallel with the application, with pause times in low milliseconds independent of heap size. Evolved from Brooks-style forwarding-pointer indirection on every read (JDK 12) to load reference barriers (JDK 13).

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Red Hat / OpenJDK's **Shenandoah** is a concurrent compacting collector that performs object relocation in parallel with the application, with pause times in low milliseconds independent of heap size.

sections.title: Barrier evolution — Brooks pointer to load reference barrier
sections.content:
Shenandoah's barrier design evolved across JDK releases:

- **JDK 12**: Brooks-style **forwarding-pointer indirection** on *every read* — every object had a forwarding word, and each read followed it. High overhead on primitive-heavy workloads.
- **JDK 13**: **Load reference barriers** that fire only at reference-load definition sites, eliminating primitive-read overhead. Crucial because most reads in Java code are primitive field accesses, not reference loads.
- **JDK 14**: **Self-fixing barriers** — the slow path CAS-updates the field back to the to-space copy, so each location pays the slow path at most once.

The progression from Brooks pointer to load reference barrier to self-fixing barrier is the canonical example of **barrier amortisation**: each generation of the collector pushed barrier cost from per-read to per-reference-read to once-per-field-per-cycle.

sections.title: Connection-matrix region tracking
sections.content:
Connection-matrix region tracking instead of card-table-style remembered sets. Each pair of regions (i,j) gets a single bit indicating whether region i contains references into region j; the matrix is updated by write barriers and read by the collector to determine cross-region root sets.

Supports heaps up to ~4 TB; **generational mode** (JEP 404) introduced experimentally.

sections.title: Status and deployment
sections.content:
Status (as of 2026-04): production-ready since JDK 15; default in Red Hat builds; widely used for latency-sensitive Java workloads. Notably absent from Oracle JDK by default but available via OpenJDK distributions.

Shenandoah and ZGC occupy adjacent niches in the JVM low-latency story: Shenandoah uses Brooks-style forwarding plus load reference barriers; ZGC uses colored pointers plus load barriers. Both are direct heirs of Azul C4.
